Name: Pascha
Also Known As: Easter; Feast of Feasts; Resurrection of Christ; Bright Sunday
Fasting Notes: Bright Week that follows is entirely fast-free; the day itself ends the Great Fast of Lent.
Brief: The Resurrection of Christ from the dead, the center and crown of the whole Church year, celebrated as the passage from death to life. It is not counted among the Twelve Great Feasts but stands above them as the "Feast of Feasts."
Customs & Traditions: The midnight Paschal service with the procession and the cry "Christ is risen!"; the blessing of Paschal baskets with meat, eggs, cheese, and bread; the greeting "Christ is risen — Truly He is risen!" exchanged through Bright Week; red-dyed eggs.
Notes: Apodosis of Pascha falls on the Wednesday before Ascension (P+38), the Leavetaking of the feast.

Name: Nativity of the Theotokos
Also Known As: Nativity of the Virgin Mary; Birth of the Mother of God
Brief: The birth of the Virgin Mary to the aged and childless Joachim and Anna, who conceived her after long prayer. It opens the Church year (which begins Sep 1) with the coming into the world of the one who would bear Christ.
Customs & Traditions: The first Great Feast of the Church year, kept with festal Liturgy and, in many parishes, the Artoklasia (blessing of five loaves) in thanksgiving. Coming at harvest time it carries first-fruits associations, and it is a major patronal 'Panagia' festival and name-day for Maria, Panagiotis, and Despina.
Notes: First of the Twelve Great Feasts in the fixed calendar year.

Name: Exaltation of the Holy Cross
Also Known As: Universal Exaltation of the Precious and Life-Giving Cross; Elevation of the Cross
Fasting Notes: A day of strict fasting even though it is a Great Feast, in honor of the Cross and the Lord's Passion.
Brief: Commemorates the finding of the True Cross in Jerusalem by the Empress Helen and its solemn elevation before the people, together with its later recovery from Persia. The Cross is lifted up and venerated as the instrument of salvation.
Customs & Traditions: The solemn elevation of a cross adorned with basil by the clergy, with repeated "Lord, have mercy"; veneration of the Cross by the faithful; strict fasting kept on the feast.
Notes: One of the strict-fast Great Feasts.

Name: Entry of the Theotokos into the Temple
Also Known As: Presentation of the Virgin Mary in the Temple; Entrance of the Mother of God
Brief: The presentation of the child Mary in the Temple at Jerusalem by her parents Joachim and Anna, where by tradition she was received by the priest Zechariah and dwelt in preparation for her role as the living temple of God.
Customs & Traditions: In many places the feast opens the singing of the Nativity katavasiae ("Christ is born"), pointing the faithful toward the coming Nativity fast. On its eve some Greek parishes bless the polysporia, a dish of many boiled seeds and grains, honoring the Theotokos as 'Mesosporitissa,' protectress of the newly-sown fields.
Notes: Falls within the Nativity Fast.

Name: Nativity of Christ
Also Known As: Nativity of the Lord; Christmas; Nativity in the Flesh of Our Lord
Fasting Notes: The feast day ends the forty-day Nativity Fast; the days from the feast to the eve of Theophany are kept fast-free.
Brief: The birth of Jesus Christ in the flesh in Bethlehem of the Virgin Mary, God taking on human nature for the salvation of the world. It is called by the Church the "mother of all feasts."
Customs & Traditions: A strict-fast Eve (Paramony) kept until the first star, with the Royal Hours, closing the forty-day Nativity Fast. Home customs abound: Greek families bake the Christopsomo ('Christ's bread') and sing the kalanda, Slavic households keep the meatless Holy Supper of twelve dishes centered on kutia, Serbs bring in the Badnjak and break the coin-hidden cesnica, and Romanians carry the colinde from house to house. (In Greek and Serbian custom, gift-giving waits for St Basil on Jan 1.)
Notes: Preceded by the forty-day Nativity Fast; its Eve (Dec 24) is a strict fast.

Name: Theophany
Also Known As: Epiphany; Baptism of the Lord; Holy Theophany
Fasting Notes: The feast day itself is fast-free; its Eve (Jan 5) is a day of strict fasting.
Brief: The baptism of Christ in the Jordan by John the Forerunner, at which the voice of the Father and the descent of the Spirit as a dove revealed the Holy Trinity. "Theophany" means the shining-forth of God.
Customs & Traditions: The Great Blessing of Waters, served both on the Eve and on the feast, often at a river, lake, or sea, with the plunging of the cross and (in many places) swimmers retrieving it; blessing of homes with the newly sanctified water.
Notes: The Eve (Jan 5) is a strict fast.

Name: Meeting of the Lord in the Temple
Also Known As: Presentation of the Lord; The Meeting; Candlemas; Hypapante
Brief: The presentation of the forty-day-old Christ in the Temple, where the aged Simeon received Him in his arms and Anna the prophetess proclaimed Him, forty days after the Nativity.
Customs & Traditions: In some traditions candles are blessed on this feast (the Western name "Candlemas"), carried and lit as a sign of Christ the Light meeting His people.
Notes: Apodosis is normally Feb 9, but the afterfeast is shortened when the Triodion begins early, so its leavetaking can fall as soon as the day after the feast.

Name: Annunciation of the Theotokos
Also Known As: Annunciation of the Virgin Mary; Annunciation to the Mother of God
Fasting Notes: Falls during Great Lent; fish is permitted on the feast day in honor of the Incarnation. When it falls in Holy Week the fasting rule is adjusted.
Brief: The archangel Gabriel's announcement to the Virgin Mary that she would conceive and bear the Son of God, and her free assent, "Be it unto me according to thy word." It falls nine months before the Nativity.
Customs & Traditions: Fish is blessed for the table even in Lent, and where the feast coincides with the Liturgy the joy of the Incarnation is kept despite the fast — in Greece the day's fish is the traditional bakaliaros skordalia (fried salt cod with garlic sauce). In Greek lands the feast also falls on Independence Day (Mar 25), joining the Liturgy to national doxologies and parades, and it is a name-day for Evangelos and Evangelia.
Notes: Its Leavetaking (Mar 26) is the Synaxis of the Archangel Gabriel; Great Lent leaves no ordinary afterfeast.

Name: Entry of the Lord into Jerusalem
Also Known As: Palm Sunday; Entry into Jerusalem; Willow Sunday
Fasting Notes: A Great Feast within Great Lent on which fish is permitted, though the strict fast resumes for Holy Week that follows.
Brief: Christ's triumphal entry into Jerusalem riding on a donkey, greeted by crowds spreading their garments and palm branches and crying "Hosanna," days before His Passion.
Customs & Traditions: The blessing and distribution of palms and pussy-willow branches held by the faithful with lit candles at the festal service; in Slavic lands willow branches are used, giving the day the name "Willow Sunday."
Notes: Has no forefeast or apodosis, standing between Lazarus Saturday and Holy Week.

Name: Ascension of the Lord
Also Known As: Ascension of Christ; Holy Ascension
Brief: Forty days after Pascha, Christ's ascent into heaven from the Mount of Olives in the presence of the Apostles, taking up human nature to the right hand of the Father.
Customs & Traditions: On the eve of the feast the Church keeps the Leave-taking of Pascha: the Paschal hymns are sung and 'Christ is risen!' is exchanged for the last time in the season, and in the Slavic use the Epitaphios, kept on the altar since Pascha, is carried in procession and laid away.
Notes: Always a Thursday, forty days after Pascha. Its afterfeast runs eight days to the Apodosis on the Friday before Pentecost (P+47); it has no forefeast, its eve being the Leavetaking of Pascha.

Name: Pentecost
Also Known As: Holy Pentecost; Descent of the Holy Spirit; Trinity Sunday
Fasting Notes: The week following Pentecost is entirely fast-free, even on Wednesday and Friday.
Brief: Fifty days after Pascha, the descent of the Holy Spirit upon the Apostles in tongues of fire, the birth of the Church and the completion of the Paschal season. The following day honors the Holy Spirit.
Customs & Traditions: The kneeling Vespers of Pentecost, at which the faithful kneel for the first time since Pascha for the long prayers of the Spirit; churches and homes adorned with green branches and flowers.
Notes: Its afterfeast runs to the Apodosis on the following Saturday (P+55); the Monday after is the Day of the Holy Spirit.

Name: Transfiguration of the Lord
Also Known As: Transfiguration of Christ; Metamorphosis
Fasting Notes: Falls within the Dormition Fast; fish is permitted on the feast day.
Brief: Christ's transfiguration on the mountain before Peter, James, and John, His face and garments shining with uncreated light while Moses and Elijah appeared with Him and the Father's voice was heard.
Customs & Traditions: The blessing of the first grapes and other fruits of the new harvest, brought to the church to be sanctified on the feast; in Russian practice, where grapes were scarce, apples are blessed instead - the beloved 'Apple Savior' (Yablochny Spas).
Notes: Falls within the Dormition Fast (Aug 1-14).

Name: Dormition of the Theotokos
Also Known As: Dormition of the Mother of God; Falling-Asleep of the Virgin Mary; Assumption
Fasting Notes: Concludes the two-week Dormition Fast; fish is permitted on the feast day.
Brief: The falling-asleep (repose) of the Virgin Mary and, by the Church's tradition, the taking up of her body to her Son. It is the last of the Twelve Great Feasts in the Church year.
Customs & Traditions: Preceded by the two-week Dormition Fast with the Paraklesis canons sung to the Theotokos; the blessing of flowers and fragrant herbs, and in some places a burial-shroud (epitaphios) procession of the Theotokos. In Greece it is the great feast of summer (the Dekapentavgousto) and a major name-day for all named after the Panagia, with the celebrated pilgrimage to the wonderworking icon on Tinos.
Notes: Preceded by the Dormition Fast (Aug 1-14).

Name: Great Lent
Also Known As: Great Fast; The Forty Days; Holy Forty Days; Tessarakoste
Fasting Notes: The most demanding fast of the year, kept as a graded discipline: meat, dairy, eggs, fish, wine, and oil are given up, with wine and oil traditionally allowed on weekends and certain feasts, and fish permitted only on the Annunciation and Palm Sunday. Observance varies by day and by pastoral counsel.
Brief: The chief penitential season of the Church year, a springtime of the soul preparing the faithful for Pascha through intensified prayer, almsgiving, and fasting in imitation of Christ's forty days in the wilderness.
Customs & Traditions: Forgiveness (Cheesefare) Vespers on the eve of Clean Monday opens the fast with mutual pardon; the Great Canon of St Andrew of Crete and the Presanctified Liturgy are served on weekdays; Saturdays of the Dead (Soul Saturdays) with kolyva commemorate the departed.
Notes: The Forty Days proper run from Clean Monday through the Friday before Lazarus Saturday; Lazarus Saturday, Palm Sunday, and Holy Week fall outside the count and are kept as their own observances.

Name: Holy Week Fast
Also Known As: Great and Holy Week; Passion Week
Fasting Notes: The strictest fasting of the entire year: the faithful traditionally keep a strict (xerophagy) fast, with many observing an especially rigorous fast on Great Friday and Great Saturday. Observance varies with pastoral counsel.
Brief: The week following Palm Sunday in which the Church accompanies Christ through His Passion, Crucifixion, and burial, culminating at the threshold of Pascha.
Customs & Traditions: The Bridegroom Matins of the first three days, the Holy Thursday commemoration of the Mystical Supper, the Twelve Passion Gospels and the taking-out of the Epitaphios on Holy Friday, and the Lamentations at the Tomb.
Notes: Kept distinct from the Forty Days of Great Lent though continuous with it; the fast reaches its peak here.

Name: Apostles' Fast
Also Known As: Fast of the Holy Apostles; Peter and Paul Fast; Apostles' Lent
Fasting Notes: A lighter fast than Great Lent: fish, wine, and oil are permitted on most days, with the usual abstention on Wednesdays and Fridays. Observance varies with pastoral counsel.
Brief: A fast in honor of the chief apostles Peter and Paul, following the descent of the Holy Spirit and preparing the faithful for their feast on June 29.
Customs & Traditions: Kept in thanksgiving for the apostles Peter and Paul and in imitation of the fast they observed before setting out to preach; a gentler fast whose length changes each year with the date of Pascha.
Notes: Variable in length because it begins on a movable date (the Monday after All Saints Sunday) but ends on a fixed date; in years when Pascha falls late, the fast is short and on the New (Revised Julian) Calendar can shrink to nothing.

Name: Dormition Fast
Also Known As: Fast of the Theotokos; August Fast
Fasting Notes: A short but strict fast on the pattern of Great Lent: fish is permitted only on the Transfiguration (Aug 6), with wine and oil traditionally allowed on weekends. Observance varies with pastoral counsel.
Brief: A two-week fast honoring the Most Holy Theotokos in preparation for the feast of her Dormition on August 15.
Customs & Traditions: The Paraklesis (Supplicatory Canon) to the Theotokos is chanted on the evenings of the fast; the blessing of grapes and first fruits accompanies the Transfiguration.

Name: Nativity Fast
Also Known As: St Philip's Fast; Advent Fast; Winter Lent; Forty Days of Christmas
Fasting Notes: A forty-day fast lighter than Great Lent in its earlier weeks, with fish, wine, and oil traditionally permitted on many days until the stricter final stretch (from about Dec 20). Observance varies with pastoral counsel.
Brief: A forty-day fast preparing the faithful for the Nativity of Christ, calling for repentance and almsgiving as the world awaits the Incarnation.
Customs & Traditions: Called St Philip's Fast because it begins the day after the feast of the Apostle Philip (Nov 14); the Nativity paramony (Eve, Dec 24) is kept as a strict day.

Name: Eve of Theophany
Also Known As: Paramony of Theophany; Theophany Eve; Eve of the Baptism of the Lord
Fasting Notes: A strict one-day fast (xerophagy) kept on the eve of the feast until the Royal Hours, Vesperal Liturgy, and Great Blessing of Waters are completed.
Brief: The strict fast day preceding the feast of Theophany, marked by the Royal Hours and the first Great Blessing of Waters.
Customs & Traditions: The Great Blessing of Waters is celebrated, and the faithful receive the newly-blessed holy water; in many places homes are blessed during the Theophany season.
Notes: Falls within the otherwise fast-free Twelve Days; the paramony is the exception to that fast-free period.

Name: Beheading of St John the Baptist
Also Known As: Beheading of the Forerunner; Beheading of the Honorable Head of John the Baptist; Decollation of the Forerunner
Fasting Notes: A strict fast day though a feast: the faithful abstain from meat, dairy, fish, wine, and oil in sober remembrance of the Forerunner's martyrdom, and by pious custom avoid foods eaten off a platter or cut with a knife.
Brief: A feast that is at the same time a strict fast, commemorating the unjust beheading of St John the Baptist and Forerunner at the command of Herod.
Customs & Traditions: A strict fast is kept out of mourning for the Forerunner; by custom the faithful refrain from using a knife or eating round foods and dishes served on a platter, recalling the head brought on a charger.
Notes: One of the Church's fixed fast days that coincides with a feast.

Name: Nativity-to-Theophany Fast-Free Period
Also Known As: The Twelve Days of Christmas; Svyatki; Christmastide; Holy Days
Fasting Notes: No fasting is kept, including on the Wednesdays and Fridays that fall within the period, in celebration of the Nativity.
Brief: The joyful fast-free days from the Nativity of Christ through the eve of Theophany, when the Church sets aside fasting to rejoice in the Incarnation.
Customs & Traditions: Known in Slavic practice as Svyatki (the Holy Days); homes and communities keep festal celebration between the two great feasts of the Incarnation and the Baptism.
Notes: The period ends before the strict paramony on Jan 5; some reckonings run the fast-free days through Jan 4 and treat the Eve of Theophany separately.

Name: Week after the Publican and the Pharisee
Also Known As: Solid Week; Continuous Week; Pre-Lenten Fast-Free Week; Sploshnaya Sedmitsa
Fasting Notes: No fasting is kept, including on the Wednesday and Friday of this week, as a deliberate rebuke to the pride of the Pharisee who boasted of his fasting.
Brief: The first fast-free week of the pre-Lenten season, following the Sunday of the Publican and the Pharisee, when the customary Wednesday and Friday fast is lifted to teach humility over outward observance.
Customs & Traditions: The relaxation answers the Gospel of the Sunday: the Church loosens the fast lest the faithful trust in fasting as the Pharisee did.
Notes: Runs from the Monday after the Sunday of the Publican and the Pharisee (P-70) through the Sunday of the Prodigal Son (P-63).

Name: Trinity Week
Also Known As: Week after Pentecost; Sedmitsa after Pentecost
Fasting Notes: No fasting is kept, including on the Wednesday and Friday of this week, in continued celebration of the descent of the Holy Spirit.
Brief: The fast-free week following Pentecost, when the Church rejoices in the gift of the Holy Spirit before resuming the fasting cycle with the Apostles' Fast.
Customs & Traditions: Kneeling prayers are offered at Vespers on Pentecost itself; the festal week that follows is kept fast-free until the Sunday of All Saints closes it.
Notes: Ends on the Sunday of All Saints (P+56); the Apostles' Fast begins the next day.

Name: Cheesefare Week
Also Known As: Dairy Week; Butter Week; Maslenitsa; Week of the Last Judgment
Fasting Notes: A preparatory graded fast: meat is no longer eaten, but dairy, eggs, and cheese are permitted every day of the week, including Wednesday and Friday, easing the faithful toward the strictness of Great Lent.
Brief: The final pre-Lenten week, following Meatfare Sunday, in which the Church withdraws meat while still permitting dairy, gently preparing body and soul for the Great Fast.
Customs & Traditions: Known in Slavic practice as Maslenitsa (Butter Week); it closes with Forgiveness (Cheesefare) Sunday and the Vespers of Forgiveness that open Great Lent.
Notes: Begins the Monday after Meatfare (Last Judgment) Sunday and ends on Forgiveness (Cheesefare) Sunday (P-49), the day before Clean Monday.

Name: Zacchaeus Sunday
Also Known As: Sunday of Zacchaeus
Brief: The Sunday whose Gospel (Luke 19:1-10) recounts the tax-collector Zacchaeus, who climbed a sycamore tree to see Christ and welcomed Him with joy. In Slavic usage it opens the approach to the Triodion, setting the tone of longing for God that precedes repentance.
Notes: Precedes the Triodion proper; a thematic Sunday chiefly in Slavic practice.

Name: Sunday of the Publican and the Pharisee
Also Known As: Publican and Pharisee
Brief: The first Sunday of the Lenten Triodion. Its Gospel parable (Luke 18:10-14) contrasts the self-righteous Pharisee with the humble, repentant publican, teaching that God justifies the lowly of heart.
Customs & Traditions: The Lenten Triodion book is opened at the services.
Notes: Opens the Triodion.

Name: Sunday of the Prodigal Son
Also Known As: Prodigal Son
Brief: The second Sunday of the Triodion, whose Gospel (Luke 15:11-32) tells the parable of the prodigal son and the Father's merciful embrace of the returning sinner.
Customs & Traditions: Psalm 136 (137), 'By the rivers of Babylon,' is added at Matins on this and the following two Sundays.

Name: Saturday of Souls
Also Known As: Meatfare Saturday; Saturday of the Dead
Brief: The great universal commemoration of all the faithful departed, kept on the Saturday before Meatfare Sunday. The Church prays for all Orthodox Christians fallen asleep in the hope of resurrection, on the eve of the theme of the Last Judgment.
Customs & Traditions: Kolyva (boiled wheat) is offered and blessed, and memorial services are served for the departed.
Notes: One of the year's principal Soul Saturdays.

Name: Meatfare Sunday
Also Known As: Sunday of the Last Judgment
Brief: The Sunday whose Gospel (Matthew 25:31-46) proclaims Christ's Last Judgment, when the sheep are parted from the goats according to their works of mercy. It is the last day meat is eaten before Pascha.
Customs & Traditions: The last day for eating meat before Great Lent.

Name: Cheesefare Saturday
Also Known As: Saturday of the Ascetics
Brief: The Saturday before Forgiveness Sunday, commemorating all the venerable and God-bearing ascetic Fathers and Mothers who shone in the monastic life, set before the faithful as guides on the threshold of Great Lent.

Name: Forgiveness Sunday
Also Known As: Cheesefare Sunday; Sunday of the Expulsion of Adam
Brief: The last Sunday before Great Lent, recalling the expulsion of Adam from Paradise; the Gospel (Matthew 6:14-21) teaches mutual forgiveness and hidden fasting. The faithful enter the fast reconciled to one another.
Customs & Traditions: The rite of mutual forgiveness (Forgiveness Vespers) is served in the evening; the last day for dairy before the fast.

Name: Clean Monday
Also Known As: Pure Monday; Kathara Deftera
Brief: The first day of Great Lent, so named for the 'clean' beginning of the fast and the spiritual house-cleaning of repentance it inaugurates.
Customs & Traditions: The Great Canon of St Andrew of Crete begins to be read at Great Compline through the first four evenings of Lent.
Notes: First day of Great Lent (the fast-season row is owned separately).

Name: Saturday of St Theodore the Recruit
Also Known As: First Saturday of Great Lent; Saturday of the Kolyva Miracle
Brief: The Saturday of the first week of Great Lent, keeping the memory of the miracle of St Theodore the Recruit, who warned the faithful of the emperor Julian's plot to defile the market food and bade them eat boiled wheat instead.
Customs & Traditions: Kolyva (boiled wheat) is blessed in his honor and distributed to the faithful.

Name: Sunday of Orthodoxy
Also Known As: First Sunday of Great Lent; Triumph of Orthodoxy
Brief: The first Sunday of Great Lent, commemorating the restoration of the holy icons in 843 and the final triumph over iconoclasm, a celebration of the Church's confession of the true faith of the Ecumenical Councils.
Customs & Traditions: A procession with the holy icons is held and the Synodikon of Orthodoxy is proclaimed.

Name: Second Saturday of Souls of Great Lent
Also Known As: Soul Saturday of the Second Week
Brief: The first of the three Lenten Soul Saturdays, on which the Church again prays for all the departed faithful, extending the mercy sought in the fast to those who have fallen asleep.
Customs & Traditions: Kolyva is offered and memorial services are served for the departed.
Notes: Soul Saturday of the second week of Lent.

Name: Sunday of St Gregory Palamas
Also Known As: Second Sunday of Great Lent
Brief: The second Sunday of Great Lent, honoring St Gregory Palamas, archbishop of Thessalonica, who defended the reality of the uncreated divine light and the prayer of the heart. His commemoration is seen as a continuation of the triumph of Orthodoxy.

Name: Third Saturday of Souls of Great Lent
Also Known As: Soul Saturday of the Third Week
Brief: The second of the three Lenten Soul Saturdays, continuing the Church's prayer for all the departed through the weeks of the fast.
Customs & Traditions: Kolyva is offered and memorial services are served for the departed.
Notes: Soul Saturday of the third week of Lent.

Name: Sunday of the Veneration of the Cross
Also Known As: Third Sunday of Great Lent; Adoration of the Cross
Brief: The third Sunday of Great Lent, at the midpoint of the fast, when the Cross is brought out for veneration to strengthen and encourage the faithful for the remainder of Lent as the Lord's Passion draws near.
Customs & Traditions: The Cross is solemnly brought out and venerated, often adorned with flowers, at the end of Matins.

Name: Fourth Saturday of Souls of Great Lent
Also Known As: Soul Saturday of the Fourth Week
Brief: The last of the three Lenten Soul Saturdays (of the second, third, and fourth weeks), on which the Church makes her final Lenten commemoration of all the departed faithful.
Customs & Traditions: Kolyva is offered and memorial services are served for the departed.
Notes: Soul Saturday of the fourth week of Lent.

Name: Sunday of St John Climacus
Also Known As: Fourth Sunday of Great Lent; John of the Ladder
Brief: The fourth Sunday of Great Lent, honoring St John Climacus, abbot of Sinai and author of 'The Ladder of Divine Ascent,' whose steps of virtue are held before the faithful as the pattern of ascetic struggle in the second half of the fast.

Name: Saturday of the Akathist
Also Known As: Akathist Saturday; Salutations to the Theotokos
Brief: The Saturday of the fifth week of Great Lent, on which the whole Akathist Hymn to the Most Holy Theotokos is chanted in thanksgiving for her protection of the Church.
Customs & Traditions: The Akathist Hymn to the Theotokos (the 'Salutations') is sung in full; it is also chanted in four stanzas on the first four Fridays of Lent.

Name: Sunday of St Mary of Egypt
Also Known As: Fifth Sunday of Great Lent
Brief: The fifth Sunday of Great Lent, honoring St Mary of Egypt, the former harlot who repented utterly and lived forty-seven years in the desert beyond the Jordan, set before the faithful as the supreme image of repentance as the fast nears its end.
Notes: Last Sunday of Great Lent before Lazarus Saturday and Palm Sunday (owned by another agent).

Name: Lazarus Saturday
Also Known As: Saturday of the Righteous Lazarus; Saturday of Lazarus
Brief: The day the Church commemorates Christ raising his friend Lazarus from the tomb after four days, a foreshadowing of the general resurrection and the Lord's own rising. It falls the day before Palm Sunday and marks the close of Great Lent proper before Holy Week begins.
Customs & Traditions: In the Greek tradition sweet spiced breads shaped like a swaddled figure ('Lazarakia') are baked, and children sing carols recalling the raising of Lazarus.
Notes: Distinct from the individual feast of St. Lazarus the Four-Days-Dead (Oct 17), which shares the same saint.

Name: Great and Holy Monday
Also Known As: Holy Monday; Great Monday
Brief: The first day of Holy Week recalls Joseph the all-comely Patriarch, sold by his brothers as a type of the suffering Christ, and the barren fig tree that the Lord cursed as an image of a fruitless soul.
Customs & Traditions: The Bridegroom Matins (the service of the Bridegroom, 'Behold, the Bridegroom comes at midnight') are served in the evening, with the icon of Christ the Bridegroom set out for veneration.

Name: Great and Holy Tuesday
Also Known As: Holy Tuesday; Great Tuesday
Brief: This day brings to mind the parable of the Ten Virgins, calling the faithful to keep the lamp of the soul filled and to watch for the coming of the Bridegroom.
Customs & Traditions: The Bridegroom Matins are served again in the evening; the hymn of Kassiani is chanted at the Tuesday evening service in Greek practice.

Name: Great and Holy Wednesday
Also Known As: Holy Wednesday; Great Wednesday; Holy and Great Wednesday
Brief: The Church contrasts the sinful woman who anointed Christ's feet with costly myrrh and her tears against Judas, who on this day went to the chief priests to betray the Lord for silver.
Customs & Traditions: The Sacrament of Holy Unction is celebrated on this evening in the Greek tradition, when the faithful are anointed for healing of soul and body.

Name: Great and Holy Thursday
Also Known As: Holy Thursday; Great Thursday; Maundy Thursday
Brief: On this day the Church commemorates the Mystical Supper, at which Christ instituted the Eucharist, together with His washing of the disciples' feet, the prayer in Gethsemane, and the beginning of the betrayal.
Customs & Traditions: The Divine Liturgy of St. Basil is served in the morning; in cathedral and monastic practice the washing of feet is reenacted, and hierarchs consecrate the Holy Chrism.

Name: Great and Holy Friday
Also Known As: Holy Friday; Great Friday; Good Friday; Holy and Great Friday
Fasting Notes: A day of the strictest fasting of the year; many of the faithful keep total abstinence from food where they are able, in mourning for the crucified Lord.
Brief: The Church keeps the Passion of Christ — His trial, crucifixion, death, and burial. No Divine Liturgy is served on this most solemn day of the year.
Customs & Traditions: The Royal Hours and the Vespers of the Taking-Down from the Cross are served; the Epitaphios (the burial shroud) is brought out and carried in procession, and the faithful venerate it and pass beneath it.

Name: Great and Holy Saturday
Also Known As: Holy Saturday; Great Saturday; The Blessed Sabbath
Brief: The Church keeps the Sabbath of Christ's rest in the tomb and proclaims His descent into Hades to free the souls held there, on the eve of the Resurrection.
Customs & Traditions: The Vesperal Divine Liturgy of St. Basil is served, at which the vestments and hangings are changed from dark to white; in many places the reading of the whole Book of Acts precedes the midnight Paschal service.

Name: Bright Week
Also Known As: Renewal Week; Diakainisimos; The Week of Renewal
Fasting Notes: The entire week is fast-free, as the joy of the Resurrection permits no fasting or kneeling.
Brief: The seven days following Pascha Sunday, kept as one continuous day of Resurrection joy. The Paschal greeting 'Christ is risen! Truly He is risen!' is exchanged throughout.
Customs & Traditions: The Royal Doors of the iconostasis remain open all week as a sign of the open tomb; the Paschal (Bright Week) services are sung daily, and red eggs are shared as a sign of the Resurrection.
Notes: Runs from Bright Monday (P+1) through Bright Saturday (P+6), the week after Pascha Sunday itself.

Name: Bright Friday
Also Known As: Theotokos of the Life-Giving Spring; Life-Giving Fount; Zoodochos Pege
Brief: On the Friday of Bright Week the Church honors the Most Holy Theotokos under her title of the Life-Giving Spring, recalling the miraculous healing fountain near Constantinople associated with her intercession.
Customs & Traditions: A special service to the Life-Giving Spring is sung; in the Greek tradition holy water is blessed and the faithful are sprinkled, and the feast is kept with festal joy within Bright Week.
Notes: Falls within Bright Week (fast-free).

Name: Thomas Sunday
Also Known As: Antipascha; New Sunday; Sunday of Antipascha; Sunday of the Apostle Thomas
Brief: The first Sunday after Pascha, when the Church recalls the risen Christ appearing to the Apostle Thomas, who passed from doubt to the confession "My Lord and my God" (John 20:19-31). It is called Antipascha ("in place of Pascha") because it renews the paschal joy on the eighth day.
Customs & Traditions: Called Antipascha, the 'renewal' of the Paschal feast, recalling Christ's appearance to the doubting Thomas eight days after the Resurrection; it closes Bright Week and reopens the season of weddings, which are set aside through Lent and Bright Week.

Name: Radonitsa
Also Known As: Day of Rejoicing; Radonica; Tuesday of Thomas Week; Rejoicing of the Departed
Brief: A Slavic commemoration of the departed on the Tuesday after Thomas Sunday, when the paschal proclamation of the Resurrection is carried to the graves of the faithful. Its name derives from the word for joy, for the Church announces Christ's victory over death even at the tomb.
Customs & Traditions: After the Liturgy and memorial service, families visit the graves of their departed, greet them with "Christ is Risen," and share food at the cemetery; a panikhida is served for the reposed.

Name: Sunday of the Myrrhbearing Women
Also Known As: Sunday of the Myrrhbearers; Third Sunday of Pascha
Brief: The third Sunday of Pascha honors the women who came at dawn bearing myrrh to anoint the Lord's body and were the first to learn of His rising (Mark 15:43-16:8), together with the righteous Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus who took Him down from the Cross and buried Him.
Customs & Traditions: In many places it is kept as a day of honor for Orthodox women.

Name: Sunday of the Paralytic
Also Known As: Fourth Sunday of Pascha
Brief: The fourth Sunday of Pascha recalls Christ's healing of the man paralyzed for thirty-eight years at the Sheep's Pool of Bethesda (John 5:1-15), a sign of the risen Lord who raises up the infirm and forgives sin.

Name: Mid-Pentecost
Also Known As: Mid-Feast; Mesopentecost; Prepolovenie
Brief: Falling on the Wednesday midway between Pascha and Pentecost, this feast recalls Christ teaching in the Temple at the middle of the feast and calling the thirsty to come to Him for living water (John 7:14-30), joining the theme of the Resurrection to the coming gift of the Spirit.
Customs & Traditions: The lesser blessing of waters is traditionally served on this day.

Name: Sunday of the Samaritan Woman
Also Known As: Fifth Sunday of Pascha
Brief: The fifth Sunday of Pascha tells of Christ's meeting with the Samaritan woman at Jacob's well (John 4:5-42), to whom He revealed Himself as the Messiah and the giver of living water; she later evangelized and died a martyr, honored as St. Photini.

Name: Sunday of the Blind Man
Also Known As: Sixth Sunday of Pascha
Brief: The sixth Sunday of Pascha recounts Christ giving sight to the man born blind (John 9:1-38), who came to confess Him as Lord -- an image of the light of Christ opening the eyes of the soul.

Name: Apodosis of Pascha
Also Known As: Leavetaking of Pascha; Otdanie of Pascha
Brief: The leavetaking of Pascha on the Wednesday before Ascension, when the paschal services are celebrated one last time in their fullness and the Church bids farewell to the feast of the Resurrection before the Lord's ascent into heaven.

Name: Sunday of the Fathers of the First Ecumenical Council
Also Known As: Sunday of the 318 Fathers; Sunday of the Holy Fathers of Nicaea; Seventh Sunday of Pascha
Brief: Kept on the Sunday after Ascension, this day honors the 318 God-bearing Fathers who gathered at Nicaea in 325 to confess the Son as consubstantial with the Father and gave the Church the Creed. The Gospel is Christ's high-priestly prayer for the unity of His disciples (John 17:1-13).

Name: Saturday of Souls before Pentecost
Also Known As: Trinity Saturday; Saturday of the Dead; Psychosabbaton before Pentecost
Brief: The universal commemoration of all the departed on the eve of Pentecost, when the Church prays for the repose of the faithful of every age as she prepares to celebrate the descent of the life-giving Spirit.
Customs & Traditions: A memorial service is served and kollyva offered for all the departed.

Name: Monday of the Holy Spirit
Also Known As: Day of the Holy Spirit; Whit Monday; Spirit Monday
Brief: The day after Pentecost is dedicated to the All-Holy Spirit, honoring the third Person of the Holy Trinity who descended upon the Apostles and continues to sanctify the Church.
Customs & Traditions: Continuing the feast of Pentecost, churches and homes are adorned with green branches, foliage, and flowers, and the faithful hold greenery through the services as a sign of the Spirit renewing creation; the week that follows is fast-free. The Kneeling Vespers of Pentecost the evening before brings the first prostrations since Pascha.

Name: Sunday of All Saints
Also Known As: First Sunday after Pentecost
Brief: The first Sunday after Pentecost gathers into one commemoration all the saints of every time and place, known and unknown -- the fruit of the Holy Spirit poured out at Pentecost, and a witness to the holiness to which every Christian is called.

Name: Sunday of All Saints of the Local Church
Also Known As: All Saints of Russia; All Saints of America; All Saints of Romania; All Saints of Serbia; All Saints of Georgia; All Saints of Bulgaria; All Saints of Mount Athos
Brief: On the second Sunday after Pentecost many local Churches keep a synaxis of their own saints -- the holy men and women of a particular land or monastic mountain -- following the commemoration of all the saints the week before.
Customs & Traditions: Each local Church gathers its own holy ones in a national commemoration — All Saints of Russia, of America, of Mount Athos, and more — kept on the Sundays after Pentecost as its patronal feast of every saint of the land, known and unknown.
Tradition of Observance: Russian; Romanian; Serbian; Georgian; Bulgarian; North American
Notes: Concrete examples include All Saints of Russia, All Saints of North America, All Saints of Romania, All Saints of Serbia, All Saints of Georgia, All Saints of Bulgaria, and All Saints of Mount Athos, each kept by its local Church.

Name: Circumcision of the Lord
Also Known As: Circumcision of Christ; Feast of the Circumcision
Brief: On the eighth day after His Nativity the Lord Jesus Christ was circumcised according to the Law of Moses and received the name Jesus, willingly submitting to the Law He had given in order to fulfill it and to show the reality of His human nature.
Customs & Traditions: In the Greek tradition the day is kept together with St Basil the Great, with the cutting of the vasilopita (St Basil's bread) in which a coin is baked.
Notes: Falls on Jan 1 together with the memory of St Basil the Great, whose own commemoration is carried in the saints table (OS-0021). Not one of the Twelve Great Feasts. Jan 1 is fast-free, within the twelve days of the Nativity.

Name: Beginning of the Indiction — the Church New Year
Also Known As: Ecclesiastical New Year; Indiction; Church New Year
Brief: September 1 opens the Orthodox liturgical year. The day recalls the Lord's entrance into the synagogue at Nazareth, where He read from Isaiah and proclaimed 'the acceptable year of the Lord,' and in the Byzantine era it also marked the start of the civil tax cycle, the indiction.
Customs & Traditions: In recent decades the Ecumenical Patriarchate has set Sep 1 aside as a day of prayer for the protection of the natural creation, an observance now kept in many jurisdictions.
Notes: The name 'indiction' comes from the fifteen-year Roman tax cycle that began in September; the Church retained September 1 as the start of its year.

Name: Protection of the Most Holy Theotokos
Also Known As: Pokrov; Holy Protection; Feast of the Veil; Skepi; Intercession of the Theotokos
Brief: Commemorates the vision at the Blachernae church in Constantinople, where St Andrew the Fool-for-Christ and his disciple Epiphanius beheld the Theotokos spreading her veil (omophorion) in protection over the faithful at prayer. It is kept as a feast of the Mother of God's unfailing shelter and intercession.
Customs & Traditions: Especially beloved in the Slavic churches (Russia, Ukraine), where Pokrov is a major autumn feast. In the Greek Church the Protection is transferred to Oct 28, joined in thanksgiving to the deliverance of Greece in 1940.
Notes: Greek usage keeps the feast on Oct 28 (Agia Skepi), associated with 'Ochi Day' 1940, while the Slavic churches keep the original Oct 1. Because it is venerated across all of Orthodoxy, Tradition of Observance is left pan-Orthodox despite the strong Slavic emphasis.

Name: Procession of the Precious and Life-Giving Cross
Also Known As: Origin of the Honorable Wood of the Cross; Feast of the Cross of August 1
Fasting Notes: First day of the Dormition Fast (Aug 1–14), one of the Church's four fasting seasons; kept strictly, with wine and oil generally permitted on Saturday and Sunday.
Brief: On August 1 the Precious Cross was carried in procession through Constantinople for the health of the city during the sickly late-summer season, with a lesser blessing of water. The day also opens the two-week Dormition Fast.
Customs & Traditions: A lesser blessing of the waters is served; in Russian practice the first fruits of the new honey are also blessed (the 'Honey Savior,' Medovy Spas).
Notes: The Seven Holy Maccabee Martyrs are also commemorated on Aug 1 and are carried in the saints table.

Name: Deposition of the Robe of the Most Holy Theotokos at Blachernae
Also Known As: Placing of the Precious Robe of the Theotokos; Deposition of the Honorable Robe
Brief: Commemorates the enshrinement of the robe (maphorion) of the Theotokos in the church at Blachernae in Constantinople in the fifth century, a relic to which the imperial city ascribed its protection in times of siege.
Customs & Traditions: A Marian feast kept with Great Vespers and Vigil; historically the Robe was carried in procession around the walls of Constantinople for the city's protection. In Georgia, where a portion is treasured at Zugdidi, the relic is borne out for the faithful to venerate after the festal Liturgy.
Notes: One of two Constantinopolitan relic-feasts of the Theotokos, the other being the deposition of her cincture on Aug 31.

Name: Deposition of the Precious Cincture of the Most Holy Theotokos
Also Known As: Placing of the Sash of the Theotokos; Deposition of the Honorable Belt; the Holy Zone
Brief: Commemorates the enshrinement of the cincture (sash, belt) of the Theotokos in Constantinople. The relic, a portion of which is kept on Mount Athos, is honored for many healings, and the feast closes the Church year on its final day.
Customs & Traditions: At Vatopedi Monastery on Mount Athos, where a portion of the sash is kept, blessed ribbons touched to the relic are given to pilgrims, sought especially by the childless.
Notes: Falls on the last day of the liturgical year, the eve of the Indiction (Sep 1).

Name: Synaxis of the Archangel Michael and the Other Bodiless Powers
Also Known As: Synaxis of the Bodiless Powers; Feast of the Holy Angels; Archangels Michael and Gabriel
Brief: The principal feast of the holy angels, gathering the commemoration of the Archangel Michael, commander of the heavenly hosts, together with Gabriel and all nine ranks of the bodiless powers who stand before God and guard His people.
Customs & Traditions: The chief feast of the holy angels and a great name-day for those named Michael, Gabriel, Angelos, and Angeliki, kept with patronal festivals at the many 'Taxiarchis' churches — most famously the pilgrimage to the miraculous icon at Mantamados on Lesvos and to the Archangel of Panormitis on Symi. Among Serbs it is the beloved household slava Arandjelovdan, with the priest-blessed slava-bread and boiled wheat.
Notes: November 8 is the chief angelic feast, kept in the Feasts table because the angels are deliberately excluded from the saints table.

Name: Synaxis of the Archangel Gabriel
Brief: Kept on the day after the Annunciation, this synaxis honors the Archangel Gabriel, who announced to the Theotokos the conception of Christ and who bears God's tidings to His people throughout the Scriptures.
Customs & Traditions: Kept the day after the Annunciation to honor the archangel who announced the Incarnation, following the pattern of commemorating the chief servant of a Great Feast on the day after; it is a name-day for Gabriel and Gavriil and a patronal feast of the many churches dedicated to the Archangels.
Notes: A second synaxis of the Archangel Gabriel is kept on Jul 13. Placed the day after the Annunciation (Mar 25) because of Gabriel's role in it.

Name: Commemoration of the Miracle of the Archangel Michael at Chonae
Also Known As: Miracle at Chonae; Miracle at Colossae
Brief: Recalls the miracle at Chonae (Colossae) in Phrygia, where the Archangel Michael saved a healing spring-shrine and its devout guardian Archippus by opening a channel in the rock, turning aside a flood that pagans had loosed to destroy the holy place.
Customs & Traditions: The feast honors a holy healing spring guarded by the Archangel; the enduring custom is the blessing and drinking of holy water at churches and springs dedicated to St Michael, with veneration of his icon for protection and healing. It is a name-day for those named Michael and a patronal feast of Taxiarch (Archangel) churches.
Notes: The guardian of the shrine, Archippus of Hierapolis, is carried in the saints table (OS-0246).

Name: Sunday of the Holy Forefathers
Also Known As: Sunday of the Forefathers; Sunday of the Holy Ancestors
Brief: The second Sunday before the Nativity commemorates the forefathers of Christ according to the flesh — the patriarchs and righteous of the Old Testament from Adam onward — together with the Three Holy Youths and the Prophet Daniel, honoring the whole lineage that awaited the Messiah.
Notes: Falls in the window Dec 11–17.

Name: Sunday of the Holy Fathers before the Nativity
Also Known As: Sunday before the Nativity; Sunday of the Genealogy
Brief: The Sunday immediately before the Nativity commemorates all the righteous well-pleasing to God from Adam onward and reads the genealogy of Christ, gathering the ancestors and prophets who prepared and foretold His coming.
Notes: Falls in the window Dec 18–24.

Name: Sunday after the Nativity of Christ
Also Known As: Sunday after the Nativity — Joseph the Betrothed, David and James
Brief: The Sunday following the Nativity commemorates the kinsmen and companions of the newborn Christ — the Righteous Joseph the Betrothed, the Prophet-King David, and James the Brother of the Lord.
Notes: Falls in the window Dec 26–31. Commemorates Joseph the Betrothed (OS-0075), the Prophet-King David (OS-2421), and James the Brother of the Lord (OS-2066), who are also carried individually in the saints table. When the Nativity itself falls on a Sunday so that no Sunday intervenes before Jan 1, the commemoration is transferred to Dec 26.

Name: Sunday before the Theophany
Also Known As: Sunday before Epiphany
Brief: The Sunday preceding the Theophany prepares the faithful for the feast of the Lord's Baptism, reading of the beginning of the Gospel and the preaching of John the Baptist in the wilderness.
Notes: Falls in the window Dec 30–Jan 5.

Name: Sunday after the Theophany
Also Known As: Sunday after Epiphany
Brief: The Sunday following the Theophany reads of the Lord's withdrawal to Galilee and the beginning of His public preaching after His Baptism — 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.'
Notes: Falls in the window Jan 7–13.

Name: Sunday before the Exaltation of the Cross
Also Known As: Sunday before the Elevation of the Cross
Brief: The Sunday preceding the Exaltation of the Cross prepares the faithful for that feast, reading of the Son of Man who must be lifted up, that whoever believes in Him should have eternal life.
Notes: Falls in the window Sep 7–13.

Name: Sunday after the Exaltation of the Cross
Also Known As: Sunday after the Elevation of the Cross
Brief: The Sunday following the Exaltation reads the Lord's call to take up one's cross and follow Him, drawing out the meaning of the feast for the Christian life.
Notes: Falls in the window Sep 15–21.

Name: Saturday of Souls before Saint Demetrius
Also Known As: Demetrius Saturday of Souls; Demetrius Memorial Saturday; Dmitrievskaya Saturday
Brief: A memorial Saturday for the departed kept before the feast of the Great-martyr Demetrius. Established in Russia after the Battle of Kulikovo (1380) as a remembrance of fallen soldiers, it grew into a general commemoration of all the reposed.
Customs & Traditions: Kept especially in the Russian and other Slavic churches; a general panikhida (memorial service) is served, and families visit and tend the graves of their departed.
Notes: Anchored to the feast of the Great-martyr Demetrius of Thessaloniki (Oct 26; OS-0013), so it falls in the window Oct 19–25. A Slavic observance, not universal in the Greek calendar.

Name: Synaxis of the New Martyrs and Confessors of Russia
Also Known As: Sunday of the New Martyrs and Confessors of Russia; New Martyrs and Confessors of the Russian Church
Brief: Commemorates the great host of bishops, clergy, monastics, and laypeople of the Russian Church who suffered and died for Christ under the atheist persecution of the twentieth century, anchored to the martyrdom of Hieromartyr Vladimir, Metropolitan of Kiev, the first among them.
Notes: The feast is kept on the Sunday nearest Jan 25 (Old Style) / Feb 7 (New Style): on Feb 7 itself when that is a Sunday, otherwise the nearer Sunday before or after. The token 'Sun before Feb 8' approximates the rule by taking the Sunday on or before Feb 7. Dated from the martyrdom of Metropolitan Vladimir of Kiev on Jan 25 / Feb 7, 1918. Glorified by the Moscow Patriarchate in 2000.

Name: Placing of the Precious Robe of the Lord at Moscow
Also Known As: Deposition of the Robe (Chiton) of the Lord; Placing of the Robe of Christ
Brief: Commemorates the enshrinement in Moscow in 1625 of a portion of the Robe (Chiton) of the Lord, sent as a gift from the Shah of Persia and received by the Patriarch and Tsar with great honor; it became a source of many healings.
Customs & Traditions: At Moscow the Robe of the Lord was brought out each year from the Dormition Cathedral and set out for the faithful to venerate during the services, then returned after the Liturgy, with the hymns of the Precious Cross sung. A portion of the Lord's Robe is also venerated in Georgia, at Mtskheta.
Notes: A Russian feast of the Lord, distinct from the deposition of the robe of the Theotokos (Jul 2). Kept on Jul 10 in the OCA calendar.

Name: Synaxis of the Most Holy Theotokos
Also Known As: Synaxis of the Theotokos; Sabor of the Most Holy Theotokos
Brief: On the day after the Nativity the Church gathers to honor the Most Holy Theotokos, through whom the Word was made flesh — one of the most ancient of the feasts of the Mother of God.
Customs & Traditions: The faithful assemble (synaxis) to honor the Mother of God the day after she bore Christ, keeping the joyful second day of the Nativity season with hymns to the Theotokos alongside those of the feast.
Notes: The Synaxis the day after a Great Feast honors the chief human participant; here, the Theotokos who bore Christ.
